data.title: Intel: Wildly Bipolar Outcome
data.description:
We previously discussed Intel Corporation (INTC) and its big coming earnings day earlier this month. This was, of course, a rare public Strong Buy rating for the public site in April of 2025, and we moved to hold at is it got to about $50+ encouraging some profit taking just on the turnaround being largely price in. Now the company must execute, as the Street has bid the stock up. Well, the big day has arrived and the company just-reported earnings. Here we discuss results relative to our previously laid out expectations.

Our Expectations For Intel's Q4 2025

So what did we expect for Q4 earnings? We were anticipating results that were down from a year ago, and this was due to operational and strategic decisions with company business lines that we knew would impact comps. Moving forward for the entire year 2026, we expect growth from 2025, though expected that inflection really to start heavily in the spring and summer of the year.

Intel's Q4 2025 Earnings Revenue And Margin Expectations

For Q4, our expectations were to see some declines from last year, but to also get a guidance that showed performance stabilization in 2026. We discussed previously that we would be focused heavily on margins. Margins have been on the mend. With cost cuts and other efficiencies we thought we would see margins that hit 37% as adjusted. We were looking for revenue to come in at the higher end of expectations and were targeting $13.3-$13.9 billion, significantly higher than the midpoint of management's guide (which is $13.3 billion).

Intel's Q4 2025 Earnings EPS And Cash Flow

On the EPS front, we expected a swing back to positive territory. We expected earnings of $0.09-$0.12 per share in the quarter, which was also above the guide, though dipping from last year. Client computing we thought would be up mid-single digits in the report, largely from restructuring of the old Network and Edge segment. Overall, we expected product revenue to come in flat to up low to mid-single digits. We also expected ongoing debt reduction and deleveraging in the Q4 report and continue to expect this in 2026. We anticipated operating cash flow of $2.2-$2.5 billion and free cash flow $200-$400 million, depending on product mix and final capital expenditures. So how did we do?

Intel's Q4 2025 Earnings Performance

So, in Q4, the company delivered a surprise beat and revenues that were down 4% from the year prior as we expected. We were correct that revenues would be significantly higher than expected We have to highlight the margin outcome. It was a 210 basis point decline in adjusted gross margin to 37.9%, compared to 40% in the sequential Q3, and down from 42.1% from a year ago but better than guided and better than we expected. There were declines in R&D spending, worth noting, but operating margin was down 80 basis points to 8.8% from last year but also better than expected. The EPS line came in at $0.15 which was $0.04 better than our expectations, and again, our expectations were more bullish than most.

Now turning to segment performance, we would like to remind you that in Q1 2025, Network and Edge was integrated into client computing and the data center/AI segments. Impacting comps as well as the fact that Altera was deconsolidated in Q3. That said, in Q4, client computing saw revenue fall 7% from last year to $8.2 billion. We thought it would be a little better. Further, data centers and AI were up 9% from last year to $4.7 billion, better than we expected. And as far as Intel Foundry, it reported revenues that were up 4% to $4.5 billion. Make no mistake, it is undeniable that a lot of transitions have occurred in the last year. In fact, we still think referring to it as the “New Intel” is not necessarily incorrect, but we call in the more 'focused Intel."

Finally, our cash flow expectations were surpassed mightily. Well, in Q4 operating cash flow strong, and we saw gross capital expenditures of about $4 billion in the quarter and positive adjusted free cash flow of $2.2 billion, which was substantially better than we expected. To us, that was the biggest positive surprise of the quarter. With a cash influx and positive free cash flow, the company paid down $3.75 billion in debt in Q4. We expect further debt reduction in 2026.

Forward View On Intel Corporation And INTC Stock

This was a solid quarter overall. We were correct in being more bullish than most on the results. However, it is not always about where a company has been, but where it is going. So, what about looking ahead? We know that, coming into this report, the company was widely expected to grow EPS 70-80% each of the next 3 years, which is why shares were bid back up.

For Q1, the company expects revenue of $11.7 to $12.7 billion. This is actually below consensus at the midpoint, and could be a reason shares may fall. Hard to say as we craft this immediately after release, but that guide comes following a solid Q4 2025. Adjusted gross margin guidance of 34.5% seems light as well, though, for EPS, it is forecasted breakeven adjusted. That is also 5 cents below consensus. So, this is a disappointing guide, all things considered.

That said, we think 2026, still sees a nice inflection, but INTC shares were priced for a better outlook overall. We think shares remain at Hold, and/or some profit taking (shave some of the position) if you doubled or more is warranted here at $53.

data.title: XAR And SHLD: Chips And Security, Greenland Crisis The End Of NATO?
data.description:
Following the news that European countries are sending military units to Greenland to explore opportunities for a NATO mission on Greenland, President Trump has slammed the countries with a 10% tariff only to revert them later following a conversation with NATO leader Mark Rutte. While not immediate, the developments regarding Greenland are concerning for the stability of NATO as well as global stability. In this report, I discuss the importance of Greenland, the weakness in President Trump’s argument to take ownership of Greenland, and how that may affect U.S. and European aerospace and defense equities.

Is Greenland Really of Security Importance?

The fact that President Trump pursues ownership of Greenland is not new. President Trump already floated the idea to buy Greenland for security purposes in 2019. I believe there is a defensible case for Greenland being of security interest to the United States in the Arctic region.

Greenland is strategically positioned to monitor Russian submarine movements towards North America as well as airplane movements. The shortest path from Russia to the United States passes over the Arctic region. As a result, Greenland is strategically positioned as a linchpin for missile and early-warning defense, such as the to-be-developed Golden Dome.

Justification to Take Control of Greenland is Dangerous

One of several arguments that President Trump makes is that Denmark and European countries cannot defend Greenland from threats such as Russia and China. While that may be true, the United States taking control of Greenland is barely a solution for national and global security. As an alliance, NATO could step up defense presence to safeguard the interests of all NATO partners in the region. The President quite often portrays Europe as incapable of defending itself or Greenland. Interestingly, as I discussed in a publication in October 2025, Europe has been successfully defending itself against Russian drone incursions. As an example, we also note that while the U.S. Navy dominates warfare, countries such as the Netherlands are punching far above their weight. You could speak about subsystem leadership, where the Dutch Navy excels in radar technology through the SMART-L MM/N radar, able to provide ballistic missile detection and multi-target engagement through the APAR or Active Phased Array Radar with an overall superior sensor fusion philosophy.

So, taking control of Greenland is not a solution for national security interests.

National security interests also do not provide a justification to take control of Greenland. By the same logic, the following shifts could occur:

  • Canada takes Greenland and Alaska.
  • The United States, Mexico, and several countries in South America could claim French and Dutch overseas areas in the Caribbean.
  • Russia could take Ukraine to build a buffer between European countries and Moscow.
  • China can take Taiwan.

President Trump also implied that just because Danish ships landed on the coasts of Greenland does not mean they have any right to Greenland. That is problematic for several reasons. The first one is that this approach towards questioning the sovereignty of a country or area is the very same approach that Russia used in Ukraine. If all countries were to do that, we would have conflict zones arising in many continents today rather than tomorrow.

Secondly, the United States taking Greenland from a fellow NATO member is not a good look for the unity of NATO, which has already been dwindling, and only empowers countries like China and Russia. It is often forgotten that following the 9/11 attacks and the wars that followed in Iraq and Afghanistan, NATO allies stood with the United States. NORAD scrambled U.S. and Canadian fighter jets to patrol the U.S. skies, and following the activation of Article 5, NATO aircraft and crews flew hundreds of missions providing radar surveillance and airborne early warning in the North American airspace.

The NORAD mission is called Operation Noble Eagle and is actually still ongoing, and the NATO mission was called Operation Eagle Assist, lasting from October 2001 through 2002, and ended when the United States upgraded air defense and improved cooperation between civil and military authorities in the United States. What we quite often see is that these missions showing how the United States relied on allies and NATO have been removed from the collective memory. Going against a fellow NATO country immediately removes the unity that the US one day could count on when in need, making China and Russia a bigger threat than they already are today.

So, taking Greenland could lead to a global eruption of conflict zones, strengthen the position of the real adversaries, and be an incredibly high risk for the modern world that relies on chips, as it means that the United States and West no longer have any leverage whatsoever to convince China not to take Taiwan, which is the world’s center of chip manufacturing.

Can the United States Leave NATO?

I would say that while NATO still stands today and Greenland-related tariffs have been cancelled, the unity between European countries and the US is diminishing. President Trump has threatened to leave NATO several times, but that is easier said than done. Article 13 of the NATO treaty allows for a country to leave NATO one year after announcing withdrawal. Furthermore, under section 1250A “Limitation on withdrawal from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization” of the 2024 National Defense Authorization Act, congressional approval is required to withdraw from NATO through a two-thirds majority vote or an Act of Congress. The President can veto a decision from Congress, but that veto could be countered by a two-thirds majority in the House and the Senate. While NATO has been regarded as the United States cradling European allies, generally there is support for NATO, so leaving NATO is not easy, and we also note that the United States typically has around 80,000-100,000 U.S. troops on European soil, and those do not only satisfy the NATO security interests but also the U.S. interests for power projection and keeping allies aligned. So, the United States has a vested interest that goes beyond the security capabilities that NATO provides.

Strategic Trade Land and Resources Play a Bigger Role

So, Greenland is of interest from a security perspective. However, the bigger driver of why the United States would be interested in Greenland centers on strategic positioning and strategic resources. That is very much in the same way minerals became a focal point in the Ukraine-U.S.-Russia framework.

The following strategic elements are of interest and increasingly becoming accessible due to climate change:

  • Energy Resources
    • Oil & Gas
    • Uranium
  • Critical minerals and rare earths
    • Rare Earth Elements
    • Lithium
    • Graphite
    • Nickel
    • Cobalt
    • Copper
    • Zinc
    • Uranium
  • Shipping routes
    • Northern Sea Route, which cuts Asia-Europe transit by 30-40 percent.
    • Northwest Passage as alternative to Panama/Suez.
  • Food security
    • Fishery
    • Fresh water; The Arctic holds 20% of the world’s fresh water.

What we are generally seeing is that in the bigger picture, the national security interests are present, but the bigger reason why the United States would be interested in Greenland focuses on strategic resources, trade control, and food security. The United States has been engaged in a chip war with China, and the reality is that from a resource perspective, the United States is set to lose that chip war every day, and that is why it is important for the United States to press for export controls. The resources in Greenland are critical to semiconductor manufacturing, and effective control over Greenland provides the US access to those critical resources. Other key areas that would benefit from mineral and strategic resource control in Greenland are Aerospace and Defense, Battery and Energy Storage, AI Data Centers, and Power Infrastructure. These are all key areas in which the world is making big steps today. In some way, Greenland hosts the critical minerals that form the foundation of modern civilization’s industrial stack.

Why Push for Greenland Now?

There are several reasons why President Trump makes the push for Greenland now. From a semiconductor perspective, the United States is strategically invested in Intel (INTC), and TSMC (TSM) has an expansion path in Arizona. Access to strategic resources accelerates the path towards becoming a semiconductor and AI force. In some way, it is about bringing silicon back to the US after decades of outsourcing, which led to a high concentration in Taiwan and a dependence on regional stability.

In terms of enabling nature, Greenland can be the U.S. version of Taiwan. That is the entire template that is being followed now. That transformation is not going to happen overnight but fits with the aim of improving domestic capabilities. While the United States is seen as an advanced economy, the reality is that for advanced lithography, the country depends on Dutch EUV machines, and Intel missed the EUV train and is currently trying to catch up, while TSMC’s presence in the US and other parts of the world is strategically motivated. The only sense that the US is winning the chip race comes from export controls.

From a defense perspective, President Trump has the military momentum. The intervention in Venezuela put U.S. military power on display, and we also saw that the world generally watched the United States intervention in a sovereign country. That is not to say that the transition of power is not a good one for Venezuela, but borders and sovereignty are being blurred.

We also note that access to strategic resources has been a pain point for some aerospace and defense companies. One example is DRS, which faced some challenges in sourcing Germanium after China imposed a now-halted ban on dual-use materials. The painful reality for the U.S. military-industrial complex is that it is used as a deterrent force against the likes of Russia, China, and lately also Europe, but over 78% of the weapon systems are dependent on China-sourced minerals. So, the actual power projection is less strong than it seems.

Furthermore, using data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute, I have projected the defense budget path for Canada, the United States, and Europe. What we see is that, if nothing changes, by 2028 the United States will no longer be the dominant force measured by dollar value in the current budget path. By 2029, Europe will account for 50% of the defense budget and more than half by the end of the decade. In the White House they are likely also aware of this, and that is one of the reasons why President Trump is floating the idea of a $1.5 trillion defense budget and why pressure is being applied fast. From a budget perspective, the United States stands to lose leverage in the years ahead on the current budget trajectory.

We have seen President Trump push for higher defense spending for other NATO members for years, and while that may have been a good move if you view things as if the United States is providing some sort of welfare security service, the consequence is that an expansion of budgets in European countries will ultimately lead to a new military power block, which could shift global power relations, and that is something that President Trump had not kept in mind. It is likely also the reason why previous administrations have not aggressively sought an acceleration in defense spending; it was simply beneficial to have a lower-budget partner to control rather than forcing higher budgets and losing leverage in the process. By pushing defense spending higher for EU countries, President Trump has aligned the United States to lose some of its power projection capability, which also plays a big role in geopolitics and trade.

What Does This All Mean for the U.S. Aerospace and Defense Industry?

Generally, the push for higher defense budgets is a positive for U.S. Aerospace and Defense companies. However, with the increasing rhetoric from President Trump, we are seeing European countries increasingly being pushed away from relying on the United States. I already discussed this increase in self-reliance in a report published last year, and it also led to European defense ETFs outperforming U.S. peers for a while, although I do not believe it paves the way for structural outperformance.

I have assessed over $400 billion worth of sales for holdings in the XAR ETF, which is a quarterly rebalancing U.S. Defense ETF with a $0.35% expense ratio and a $4.7 billion AUM with holdings as shown below:

What I found is that around 70% of the sales are attributed to the United States, 10% to Europe, and 19% to other countries, some of which could include European sales, as not all companies provide the same level of granularity in geographic sales. So, $40 billion in sales exposure to Europe at least, plus contracts through the Foreign Military Sales Program. I would also not say that these sales are directly at risk. That is because even with Europe’s push to become more self-sufficient for defense needs, it constitutes a multi-year push in which the European defense industrial complex has to speed up, and reliance on U.S. products continues.

What Are the Prospects for European Defense Companies?

At the same time, I believe that the latest friction between European countries and the United States is going to push Europe even more to rebuild the European defense industry, which obviously benefits European defense stocks. However, there is a limit to that. The industry currently is not yet sized to absorb all demand from European countries, nor are the capabilities developed. We also see that there is no strong mechanism in the renewed defense focus demanding Euros in the budgets to be spent with European contractors. Europe commands 65% of the content of systems to be sourced in the EU or an associated country. That seems like a blow to U.S. defense contractors, but it is not. This rule only holds for the European Defence Industry Program, which has €1.5 billion in funding for 2025-2027. So, it is largely symbolic, as the EU likely is aware that the industry does not have the capacity or required capabilities yet to be fully independent for security needs. At the same time, part of the strategy likely also involves attracting joint ventures with U.S. companies to accelerate the development of the European industry. For that reason, I am not only bullish on the prospects of XAR, but also SHLD, which has exposure to European as well as U.S. defense stocks:

SHLD, which has a $6.9 billion AUM, a 0.5% expense ratio, and a dividend yield of 0.5% with semi-annual payments and market cap weighting, in some sense presents a mix of the stability in the U.S. defense stocks and potential high-growth opportunities for European defense stocks.

If we look at the performance between XAR, which is pure U.S. aerospace exposure; SHLD, which is blended; and WisdomTree European Defense Fund (WDEF), which is a pure play on European defense stocks, we see that all ETFs outperformed the market year-to-date with no major exposure-driven benefit for one ETF or the other.

Over a one-year period, we even note that the EU-focused ETF underperformed relative to SHLD and XAR. So, XAR and SHLD, in my view, are the better picks. What I like about XAR is that it also includes some commercial exposure through companies such as Boeing (BA), Hexcel (HXL), and HEICO (HEI.A), ATI (ATI), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), and General Dynamics (GD) on top of the pure-defense names, while SHLD includes names with potential high growth in the defense business.

Conclusion: NATO Turmoil Helps European Defense Companies

I believe that the turmoil triggered by the wishes to annex Greenland provides some support for higher stock prices for EU-based defense contractors. However, that comes with the big sidenote that for the moment, such an increase in the stock price would mostly be sentiment-driven as it does not substantially add to near-term opportunities. It just justifies a higher trading multiple as the realization is increasing that European countries need to continue striving for sovereign security solutions with a strong European defense complex. So, the Greenland crisis does not mark the end of NATO, but it does serve as a validation that Europe needs its own defense solutions, and if we look at the current budget developments, we see European rising as a defense power measured by dollars in the budget and that could eventually be a catalyst for U.S. defense equities to rally as it provides credible support for the United States to increase its defense budgets as well and maintain power as a military power to drive its geopolitical and trade agenda. It is either that, or the United States has to start viewing Europe as a partner.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: This Party Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Stating that Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) was a steal at all-time highs back in early October was probably one of my best calls of the year. In that analysis, I found explosive growth in their cloud memory segment and margin expansion. Meanwhile, the valuation was still attractive. Since then, Micron has surged an astonishing 108% as of this writing to set all-time high after all-time high. In today's article, I will be reviewing their latest earnings and will also be discussing key recent developments and where the valuation is now.

Below, it is shown that Micron's Q1 was an extraordinary quarter. Growth is picking up, and margin performance was simply jaw-dropping. While their results were impressive, Q2 guidance was perhaps even more encouraging. Top-line growth is expected to speed up, and margin expansion is set to accelerate. While there are some risks of an AI bubble forming, I would say that with the forward P/E at such a low level, there is a good margin for error. As a result, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating for Micron stock.

Financial Performance

Let's start off with an overview of the company's most recent financial results. Micron was able to generate total revenues of $13.6 billion for its FY2026 Q1. As you can see above, an inflection point for top-line growth was hit a while ago, and now Q1 represents their second straight quarter of significant acceleration. With the revenue growth rate at 57%, I would say that it's pretty clear that Micron's business is booming. Later, we'll discuss supply and see how it may be affecting the company's growth right now.

In Q1, Micron destroyed expectations as they beat them by a margin of $760.74 million. Therefore, this really was a monstrous quarter in terms of outperformance. Let's turn to their revenues by technology.

As shown above, both DRAM and NAND saw robust levels of growth. That said, it really was DRAM that took most of the spotlight, as 79% of total revenues were from this technology. With growth being 69% for DRAM in the quarter, there is no question that this is a leading driver for the company moving forward. Nonetheless, the results in NAND were no slouch and show that there is broad strength in Micron's results.

Margin Expansion Is Breathtaking

In the vast majority of Micron's business, margin expansion was simply breathtaking. In their Core Data unit, they saw just 1 percentage point of gross margin expansion YoY and actually saw some operating margin contraction. However, that is unable to offset the bullishness of the other units' margin excellence.

As you can see, margins in Cloud Memory, Mobile and Client, and Automotive and Embedded saw expansion that would be better described by percentage points rather than basis points. We'll discuss the memory shortage later, but it's pretty obvious that the pricing power of Micron has increased significantly in recent times. For the company as a whole, they saw gross margin expand by 17.3 percentage points while operating margin improved by 19.5 percentage points. These numbers are honestly off the charts.

In terms of the bottom line, things don't get less impressive. For Q1, Micron reported an adjusted EPS of $4.78, representing a growth rate of 167% YoY. This is miles above the top-line growth rate, and that reflects the breathtaking margins discussed above. In addition, they were able to fly past bottom-line expectations as well as beat them by $0.82. Moving forward, you can see that analyst consensus calls for further growth acceleration before some moderation. Do note, however, that while growth may seem low for FY2027 Q1, it is still expected to be at a whopping 126.11%.

Supply Shortages

By now, it is no secret that AI has created memory shortages, and for Micron, that's, of course, a sign of unrelenting demand. As Chris Ciaccia of Seeking Alpha writes,

"memory, in particular, high-bandwidth memory, has experienced a shortage as data centers and GPUs gobble up memory for their needs."

As a sign that this is a massive boost for margins, it was reported earlier this month that Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSC.F) were considering raising server memory prices by a staggering 70% in Q1. For Micron, that is clearly an indication that the margin strength discussed above will continue at least for the near term while supply shortages remain.

In the longer run, there are good indications that the outlook is bright. The company now forecasts that their TAM will hit $100 billion in 2028, and that represents around a 40% CAGR. Next, let's discuss how they aim to capture the opportunity at hand.

Fabrication Push

On Jan 16th, it was reported that Micron broke ground on their $100 billion memory manufacturing site in New York state. While plans for this fab started a while ago, this manufacturing push should help to alleviate supply concerns in the long run. This facility is likely to become operational in 2030, and so the company has to find a quicker solution to address shortages. That's where Taiwan comes into play.

Just a day after the groundbreaking for the New York fab, it was reported that Micron is planning to buy a fab site in Taiwan from Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation for $1.8 billion. As of now, this site is on track to provide a significant amount of DRAM wafers in CY2027 H2, and so this is expected to help with the shortage much faster than the New York fab.

At this point, it seems that Micron will continue to benefit from immense pricing power in the near term, while for the long term, they are pushing into fabrication in an attempt to secure their market share. I believe this is a winning strategy.

Q2 Outlook

Let's take a look at their Q2 financial outlook. At the $18.7 billion midpoint for revenue, Micron is implying top-line growth of 132% YoY. That is steep acceleration from Q1's 57% and is a reflection of the trends that we have discussed in today's article. For gross margin, they are expecting 68% at the midpoint. That would be a 30.1 percentage point expansion YoY. I thought Q1's YoY improvement was impressive, but this is just extraordinary profitability. For operating expenses, they are projecting $1.38 billion at the midpoint, up 32% YoY. Considering the expected top-line growth rate, this increase actually shows that corporate efficiency is set to improve. Lastly, for the bottom line, the midpoint EPS estimate of $8.42 represents a 440% increase YoY. I believe no comment is necessary for that kind of growth.

Valuation Is Unbelievably Cheap

In my previous article, Micron's forward P/E was at 11.22. Since then, there has been very modest expansion to 11.78 despite the stock's large gains, and so I believe Micron remains a bargain now.

In today's analysis, I found truly remarkable fundamentals. Their Q1 results were nearly flawless, and both the near- and longer-term outlooks have been shown to be bright as the demand for memory is relentless. With Micron benefitting from huge margins right now and planning a fabrication push to secure the long-term future of the company, I would say that they are very well positioned.

While it's obvious that EPS upward revisions have weighed on the forward P/E, I believe current levels show that there is plenty of room for multiple expansion moving forward. Note that Seeking Alpha shows Micron's non-GAAP forward P/E is at a nearly 53% discount to the IT sector as of this writing. In my view, this is enough to show that the risk/reward remains deeply in the bulls' favor.

Risks

It can be inferred that the TAM forecasts are based on the assumption that the AI boom will continue strongly over the next few years. The company's fabrication push likely also has this assumption. While this may be a fair assumption to have based on what we're seeing right now, there is some risk that a bubble is forming in AI. Just today (Jan 22nd), OpenAI (OPENAI) Chairman Bret Taylor expressed that there is “probably” a bubble. Specifically, he stated that

I think over the next few years, you’ll see a correction, you also see consolidation, but I don’t think you can get innovation without that kind of messy competition.

Therefore, while AI will ultimately become a revolutionary technology much like the internet, there will be bumps along the road. This may cause short-term dips in CAPEX confidence, and AI hardware providers like Micron could see some headwinds vis-a-vis their financial performance. Since the forward P/E discussed above is based on strong growth projections, if spending pulls back, there is a risk of stock underperformance.

That said, I do believe the incredibly low level in the multiple provides a good margin for error, and so I remain very bullish despite some risks of a bubble forming.

Takeaway

Where should I start? Back in October when I upgraded Micron to a strong buy, I envisioned strong returns and a booming business. However, the amount of momentum they are seeing now is truly beyond my expectations by a significant amount. The great thing is that while the fundamentals and outlook have improved immensely, the forward P/E ratio is little changed, and so I believe the valuation still offers an ample amount of opportunity to investors. As discussed above, there are some risks of an AI bubble forming in the near term, but I would say that the valuation is low enough to offer some comfort. Furthermore, in the long run, AI will undoubtedly be a revolutionary technology, and so even if there is near-term turbulence, Micron ultimately stands to benefit. With that in mind, I believe a reiteration of my strong buy rating is justified.

data.title: Reinsurance Group Of America: Lock In 2% 'Alpha' With The Baby Bonds
data.description:
The overall market isn't signaling any problems, but we are really scared. There has been just too much news lately. It seems that investors are more worried about the US's credibility than about equity markets. Long-term bonds have a hard time catching a rally. On the contrary, corporate debt is still priced at historically low credit spreads, and we are constantly looking for opportunities to earn extra returns without taking on too much credit risk. This is the case with Reinsurance Group of America (RGA) and its baby bonds.

Reinsurance Group of America Brief Information

RGA was founded in 1973 and is headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. The company is one of the largest life insurance reinsurers in the world and operates in over 25 countries. Its total assets for the third quarter of 2025 were close to $115 billion, and its current market capitalization is around $12.78 billion.

In the snapshot below, you can see how rating agencies evaluate RGA and its subsidiaries:

And RGA's credit ratings:

The chart shows how highly correlated RGA is with the State Street SPDR S&P Insurance ETF (KIE):

With companies like RGA, we are posting just a short review of the company so that the reader can get familiar with how safe its debt is. The sweet spot of the article is to follow as we explain where the alpha is hidden.

Baby Bonds

RGA has two baby bonds (RZB) and (RZC):

Both bonds are currently rated with an "investment-grade" credit rating: S&P rates with "BBB+", Moody's with "Baa2", and Fitch with "BBB-". RZC has a yield to worst of close to 6.31% (at $25.42).

RZC is a high-duration baby bond with a maturity date of approximately 27 years, set for 10/15/2052. The bond is reset-rate security. Its call date is 10/15/2027, with about two years to call. The bond was issued on 09/15/2022 with a coupon rate of 7.13% and a spread to the 20-year Treasury yield of approximately 3.13%. After its call date, this rate will be equal to the sum of the 5-year U.S. Treasury yield on the applicable fixed rate calculation date plus 3.456%, resetting every five years thereafter on the applicable fixed rate calculation date.

Based on market consensus:

RZC is about to reset at a nominal yield higher than 7%. If we use a 5-year yield of 4.09%, the reset rate is expected to be close to 7.55%. This high expected nominal yield, combined with the company's credibility, makes RZC a very probable redemption. In fact, if the security is not redeemed on its call date, it will most likely trade as a pinned-to-par security, gravitating around par. An investor in RZC is expected to lock in the 6.30% yield to call in less than two years. To explain how generous this yield is, we have to look at the senior bonds of the company that are not listed on the primary exchanges.

OTC Bonds

Above are shown RGA's over-the-counter bonds, rated "A1" and "Baa1". These bonds are senior to RZC and, as a general rule, are expected to trade with lower yields. We view the redemption probability of RZC as very high, given its high expected nominal yield on its reset date. The company's short-duration senior bonds yield close to 4%. The YTC of RZC comes with a spread of more than 2% for going two notches lower in credit quality within the same company. We have seen this phenomenon across many fixed-to-floating and reset-rate securities. The market seems to leave some Alpha on the table by failing to assess the risk-reward opportunity properly. For such a highly rated company as RGA, this spread has to be lower than 0.5% for such a low period of time.

Fair Value of RZC

We will set a 5% yield to call as the bond's fair value. This represents a spread of more than 0.7% to the senior notes and 1.4% above the 2-year Treasury. To reach 5% YTC, the price of RZC will have to appreciate close to 2%. Such a capital appreciation may not seem particularly spectacular, but we find the risk-adjusted Alpha to be superior to almost any exchange-traded fixed-income instrument at the moment.

Conclusion

The capital structures of well-established investment-grade issuers rarely provide "Alpha" to investors, as they are usually priced by large institutions. We have been able to capitalize on small mispricings in such exchange-traded products, and RZC appears mispriced relative to its peers. We rate it as a buy as long as its yield to worst is higher than 5% and as a sell if it reaches the yield to worst of its higher-rated brothers at around 4.2% yield to call, all else being equal.

data.title: SVOL: Some Uncertainty On The Horizon
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

When we want to monetize short-term market volatility as investors, one of the alternatives we have is to do so in an indexed manner through the Simplify Volatility Premium ETF (SVOL). By doing so, we expose ourselves to daily (short-term) results, which correspond to an exposure of between -0.2 and -0.3 (inverse) of the return on the short-term volatility futures index linked to the VIX. To achieve this exposure, the ETF trades short futures and financial options (put and call) on the VIX, seeking to exploit the carry of volatility as measured by the VIX itself. In other words, it makes money if the prices of the futures it trades fall.

To do this, it is very important to understand the position of the futures curve. This can be in contango (a normal situation, where current futures are worth less than long-term futures) or backwardation (a curve inversion, where short-term futures are more expensive than long-term futures). As these are short-term futures, the fund rolls its position to maintain its desired exposure to the volatility market. When this rolling occurs and the curve is in contango, the ETF makes money, as the market is paying it a “premium” for selling protection, and if nothing serious happens.

If, on the other hand, a specific market shock occurs and the futures curve shifts from contango to backwardation, the ETF will lose money because it will not be able to capture the volatility premium due to the shape of the futures curve. In these cases, futures prices rise sharply, which has a negative impact on a short position. The fund would sell volatility at a high price today and buy it tomorrow at a lower price, so with the rolling of the trade, it would end up losing money. That is why the shape of the VIX futures curve is so important. For these situations, the ETF management team has a call option overlay, which protects the ETF when futures prices rise sharply.

As we will see later, the VIX is currently showing some signs of backwardation at the short end of the curve. As we have seen, this is detrimental to both the price and the carry of the fund. I believe there is too much uncertainty in the short to medium term to recommend holding. With the Greenland issue unresolved and seeing the major Danish pension funds divesting themselves of all the US government debt they hold, I believe we are entering unstable territory in terms of volatility, so I recommend selling SVOL.

ETF Overview

Visually, the fund has $600 million in assets under management, spread across 35 million shares. Its unsubsidized 30-day SEC yield is 1.98%, which is somewhat low, considering that expenses have not been deducted.

Comparing market price returns with NAV returns, we can see that there are certain differences across all time periods shown in Figure 2. This indicates that it is normal for ETFs to trade at a premium or discount to NAV for operational reasons (liquidity conditions, intraday demand). It is important to bear this in mind when deciding on the optimal time to enter or exit this or any other ETF. You should always try to buy at a discount and sell your shares at a premium, although this is not always easy.

The Portfolio

The holdings show us the ETF's mixed portfolio, both in terms of volatility sales and the collateral it has to support the sale operation. In the image, we can see that 33.39% of the portfolio is long on Treasury bonds, maturing in March 2026. 32.66% is short on the CBOE VIX future, which is the leg that measures the main operation. We can also see certain ETFs from the Simplify family, such as AGGH (6.41%), NMB (6.40%), SPUC (12.57%), and QIS (9.55%). These serve to provide extra carry to the ETF's price performance and as a hedge when trading derivatives.

Payoff Profile

The fund's payment profile is very easy to understand. The dividend yield contributed to a portfolio over the last 12 months has been 19.46%, indicating that it has monetized episodes of contango in the futures curve well. It can also be seen that in the last 12 months, the dividend has fallen by 0.29%, probably caused by market volatility itself. Although it was founded in 2021, it is striking that it has never grown its dividend, which indicates the dangers of exposure to an ETF of this nature.

VIX View

Looking at the VIX chart, we can see that volatility has jumped from 16.87 to above 20 points. This recent spike is entirely due to macroeconomic uncertainty surrounding Greenland and the possible actions of the various NATO powers. This recent increase in the volatility index has a negative impact on the ETF, since, as we have seen, it short sells VIX futures. Given this cooperation and the increase in the index, the prices of futures traded by the ETF rise, which weighs on both the carry and its NAV. The key here is to analyze how long the market can remain in this situation. From my point of view, I believe that the current geopolitical situation is a long-term issue and may weigh on SVOL's profitability with episodes of this type. Therefore, I recommend selling the ETF. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Nike: Too Little Room For Error
data.description:
While talks of a turnaround for Nike, Inc. (NKE) gained traction late last year, in mid-October I initiated the stock with a sell rating. I believed that Nike wasn't out of the woods yet, with margins being weak and guidance being underwhelming. Furthermore, the valuation was elevated, and so I took a bearish stance. Since then, the stock has dropped around 3%, and so at least it is quite clear that optimism hasn't gotten much stronger. Today, I'll discuss their most recent results that were reported in December as well as go over the stock's valuation.

Below, it is shown that Q2 was generally another shaky quarter for the company. With growth being basically flat and EPS falling by over 30%, there aren't many clear signs of a turnaround. The considerable slowing of margin contraction for Q3 as per their guidance and insider buying are potentially positive signals. However, at a forward P/E of over 40, I would say that the stock is too expensive based off of where the fundamentals and near-term outlook are right now. Therefore, I have decided to hang on to my sell rating.

Growth Didn't Improve Meaningfully

Let's start with a look at Nike's top line results. For FY2026 Q2, the company reported total revenues worth $12.4 billion, representing a 1% growth rate. While this is unchanged from the previous quarter, in constant currency terms there was actually an improvement from a 1% decline to being flat in their Q2. Therefore, there was a hair of strengthening in their core business activity. While this shows their top line trajectory hasn't worsened, it also fails to show that the turnaround is gaining true momentum. The worst may be over, but questions may be asked regarding where the company goes from here. Despite the very sluggish growth rate, Nike was still able to beat consensus by $218.31 million. This shows that analysts were expecting a much worse quarter from the company.

Nike Direct Weighs On Margin

In my previous article, I expressed concerns vis-à-vis Nike's gross margin, and those worries remain after looking at their latest results. While the top line was basically flat YoY, there was quite a divide regarding their direct-to-consumer sales and their wholesale revenues. For direct revenues, they saw a 9% decline YoY in constant currency down to $4.6 billion, while on the other hand, wholesale revenues saw an 8% increase to $7.5 billion. While this doesn't seem too concerning at first, it should be recognized that direct sales usually come with much higher margins than wholesale. Since consumers are seemingly increasingly preferring to buy their products at other retailers, Nike's profitability is getting eroded in a very meaningful way.

For Q2, the company saw gross margin compression of 300 basis points to 40.6%. That is a sizeable drop and indicates that the underperformance of their direct sales channel is weighing significantly. Do note that back in Q1, there was an even worse 320 basis point contraction, and so perhaps there are some early signs of stabilization. For Q2, Nike credited the gross margin weakness mainly to higher tariffs in North America. I believe the contrast between wholesale and direct sales performance is potentially a reflection of consumers hunting for better deals through retailers. This consumer behavior is in turn likely a result of tariffs eroding confidence, and so it seems that some headwinds are present.

Operating Efficiency Stable

Operating margin is not a metric that Nike likes to refer to, but as you can see, there was a drop YoY. However, that is due to the gross margin drop and doesn't actually indicate corporate inefficiencies upon a closer look. For Q2, Nike stated that selling and administrative expenses increased by 1% YoY. Since this is mostly in line with the top line growth rate, the company's operating effectiveness really hasn't changed that much, and so the drop in operating margin may be slightly misleading in that regard. While stable efficiency is definitely better than deterioration, for a turnaround story, I would like to see more concrete signs that management is making substantial changes to improve the bottom line for investors.

The stable operating efficiency was unable to save Nike's bottom line from a considerable YoY drop. They reported a diluted EPS of $0.53, representing a decline of 32% YoY. That is a worsening from Q1's 30% drop, but they were still able to beat expectations by $0.16. In percentage terms, their bottom line actually surpassed estimates much more than their top line did, and so overall profitability could have been much worse. We'll talk about their Q3 guidance later, but you can see above that analysts see EPS returning to positive growth by Q4.

Insider Buying Could Be Bullish

When insiders buy stock, it is usually quite a bullish signal. While there was quite a bit of selling in 2025, note that in late 2025, there were some key buys. As you can see above, it really started with Independent Director Jorgen Knudstorp, but the purchases by Tim Cook and CEO Elliott Hill really made the headlines. While the fundamentals for Nike aren't looking too great right now, with insiders buying, there may be signs of a better outlook that their Q2 results don't reflect. Do note, however, that a couple million dollars for the individuals above is likely just a rounding error.

Q3 Outlook Is Mixed

In their earnings call, management stated that Q3 revenues will be down low single digits. That would be a deterioration from what we saw in Q2, and so in the near term, there really aren't many signs that the business activity will strengthen for the business as a whole.

For gross margin, they are expecting to see contraction in the range of 175 to 225 basis points. While contraction is undesirable, it does represent another slowdown in deterioration from Q2, and so there are now meaningful signs that gross margin is stabilizing a bit. Since weak margins have been a major drag on the company's profitability for quite a while, this is definitely positive news.

As for SG&A expenses, they project a low-single-digit increase. With revenues expected to drop, this may be an indication of weak efficiency. However, they stated that this will be driven in part by "investments in [their] sport offense," and since these investments may be a driver of future growth, I view the increase in expenses as unconcerning. Overall, I believe Q3 guidance is mixed, but the margin stabilization is a key development worth monitoring.

Valuation Still Questionable

Despite the ups and downs, Nike's forward P/E ratio has expanded modestly from a reading of 41.59 in my previous article to a current reading of 42.16. I believe this remains too expensive of a price to pay for Nike given the current fundamentals and near term outlook.

In Q2, Nike saw rather few signs of a meaningful turnaround in the business. After evaluating the quarterly results, the stock is quite clearly overvalued in my view. In their Q3 outlook, there are mixed signs. While margin contraction is expected to slow considerably, growth is not expected to make a turn for the better, and so that doesn't make the valuation any more justified.

However, as discussed in the insider transactions section above, the buying by Tim Cook and Nike's CEO does serve as a confidence booster that the longer term outlook for the business is brighter than implied by the Q2 results and Q3 guidance. This may convey a sense of undervaluation in the stock. Do note that while insider buying is definitely a welcomed sign, purchases of a couple million dollars for these individuals likely represent a minuscule amount of their net worth. Investors should keep that in mind when considering Nike stock.

Conclusion

Currently, I would say that there are only very early signs that Nike is making a comeback. If you dig deep into their results and guidance, you do find some traces of hope. However, at the current valuation, I would say that there is too little room for error. A forward P/E of over 40 for a company that's basically seeing zero top line growth shows that investors are quite optimistic for a recovery. While insider buying is a confidence booster, I believe a couple million dollars for these folks is a rounding error, and so I would caution against concluding that the stock is undervalued just based off of these transactions. In my view, at the current valuation, investors are pricing in a very successful turnaround, and since that really hasn't materialized, I have decided to reiterate my sell rating.

data.title: Trump-Greenland Is A Distraction: Look At Japan
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Markets are currently obsessed with the Trump-Greenland headlines.

But if you ask me, the real threat isn't a territorial dispute in the North Atlantic; it’s Japan.

The Japanese economy, long the world’s reliable source of zero-cost capital, is entering a period of violent structural stress.

We are seeing a Catch-22 play out in real time where government bond yields are ripping higher, the yen is sliding toward 160, and the Bank of Japan is trapped between saving the currency or saving the bond market.

This isn’t just limited to Japan, and it’s a struggle that a lot of developed economies are facing.

Ultimately, the stage is set for an economy and market that are going to be much more difficult to navigate in the years ahead.

Japan’s Fiscal Tightrope

The Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi is doubling down on her plans to increase fiscal stimulus in the country.

Her Sanae-nomics platform promises zero-percent food taxes and a definitive end to excessive austerity.

This seems like a high-stakes gamble, considering Japan is the world’s most indebted developed economy.

The snap election scheduled for February 8, 2026, has turned this into a Truss-style fiscal panic.

Investors are, perhaps rightfully, terrified that a parliamentary majority will give Takaichi a mandate to flood the market with even more debt.

Is Japan approaching a 2022 UK Gilt-style crisis?

JGBs Are Flashing A Warning Sign

For a few months now, analysts have begun to point out the fact that Japanese bonds keep climbing.

Investors showed concern when we crossed 3% in the 40-year period, which was barely six months ago.

We’re now approaching 4%, with no signs of stopping. There seems to be a consensus among macro investors that these yields are a risk factor for equities.

But when will we actually reach a tipping point?

By themselves, raising yields doesn’t necessarily have to be so bad, but combined with a rapidly depreciating currency, they put the BoJ in a tough spot.

The yen has depreciated constantly in the last year, and this is becoming problematic.

The BoJ’s Catch-22

The Bank of Japan is now trapped between a rock and a hard place.

For some time, the BoJ has been trying to hike rates in an attempt to curb inflation and normalize policy.

But now the BoJ is expected to keep rates steady ahead of the February elections. While supporting the yen is important, the risk of triggering further weakness in bonds now arguably outweighs this.

Efforts aimed at halting the yen’s weakness through rate hikes risk fueling the upward momentum of long-term rates, which is already gaining significant speed,” Kobayashi said.

Source: Shinichiro Kobayashi, chief economist at Mitsubishi UFJ Research & Consulting.

In other words, the BoJ is clearly trying to steer monetary policy in one direction, but the government isn’t on the same page.

We’re All In The Same Boat

What we are seeing in Japan is not unlike what we are seeing in the US, and even in many other economies.

There’s a clear trend of increased fiscal spending, which can be partly attributed to the recent move to deglobalize the economy.

Countries around the world, with the US leading the way, are focusing on re-industrializing their economies, increasing defense budgets, and overall preparing for a much more fractured world economy.

Ultimately, we’re setting the stage for a decade that is going to look very different from the last one.

Japan could arguably be the first domino to fall. The danger here is that the country enters a vicious cycle of a weaker yen, higher inflation, and higher rates.

The BoJ could ultimately lose the ability to keep a lid on rates if the currency weakens too fast.

What Does This Mean For Investors?

Ultimately, currencies around the world are going to begin to suffer more and more as the fiscal burden increases.

Central banks, in my opinion, will simply have no choice but to try to accommodate this new fiscal paradigm.

The evidence is already here.

Gold and Silver are at all-time highs, defense stocks are rallying, and so are those linked to strategic assets and materials that will become more important in a more de-globalized world.

Simple index investing, especially with indexes so skewed towards a few large companies, may not be the best way to invest in the coming years or even decades.

Final Thoughts

Investors will have to learn to be a lot more selective about the stocks they own, and there’s a very real risk in the more immediate term that stocks could suffer a shock as bond yields around the globe continue to climb.

data.title: 3M Suffers Tariff Risks - Correction Triggers Dip Buying Opportunity
data.description:
I previously covered 3M Company (NYSE:MMM) in early October 2025, discussing how the management's intensified focus on portfolio renewal had triggered the accelerated growth prospects beyond historical levels, with the new product launches expected to deliver +15% YoY sales growth in FY2025.

Combined with the streamlined operations, the improved pricing power, the raised FY2025 guidance, and the reasonable valuations, I had believed that the stock presented a well-balanced capital appreciation and dividend income prospect heading into the FQ3'25 earnings call, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the MMM stock, thanks to the dollar cost averaging opportunity after the market's overreaction to the softer FY2026 guidance and the potential Greenland-related tariff impact.

This is especially since their accelerated product launches in high-growth verticals and the ongoing productivity initiatives have triggered their accelerated path to FY2027 growth targets, with the tariff risks presenting temporary noise to its business transformation.

MMM Faces Tariff Risks In FY2026

Since my last Buy rating, MMM has went ahead and hit a new 52-week high around the $174s after the well-received FQ3'25 performance/raised FY2025 guidance, before trading sideways through Q4'25.

Well, this was before the market overreacted to the mixed FY2026 guidance after a tougher YoY comparison, resulting in a steep one-day correction of -6.9% post FQ4'25 earnings call to the $156s.

For reference, MMM has provided a relatively robust, albeit mixed bag, FY2026 guidance across:

  • adj sales organic growth by +3% YoY,
  • operating margin expansion by +0.75 points YoY at the midpoint, and
  • adj EPS of $8.60 at the midpoint (+6.6% YoY),

... compared to the FY2025 performance at +2.1% YoY, +2 points YoY, and +10.4% YoY, respectively, albeit with the operating margin expansion remaining relatively in line with the prior guidance offered in the 2025 Investor Day to ~25% by 2027 and the adj EPS growth at high single digits annual growth.

Unfortunately, MMM has seen further pessimism by highlighting the potential Greenland-related tariff impact on its FY2026 bottom-lines by up to -$70M, or the equivalent of -$0.13 per share - a number that has yet to be included in its already softer FY2026 adj EPS guidance of around $8.60 per share.

Readers must note that this also builds upon the embedded "carryover effect of the $0.20 of gross impact that we saw last year" arising from the pre-existing tariff regulations.

As a result of the uncertainties entering FY2026, with the Greenland-related tariff impact potentially negating much of MMM's tailwinds from the prior operational efficiency, the new product launches, and the bottom-line performance through FY2027 - I can understand the market's overreaction post FQ4'25 earnings call.

For now, I urge readers to monitor the developing Greenland situation for a little longer, since the latest news highlights that "President Donald Trump says the US is exploring a potential deal on Greenland after talks with NATO as he backed off threats to tariff European allies that had opposed his plans for America to acquire the island."

MMM's Renewed Growth Opportunities Through New Launches

On the other hand, I urge readers to look beyond the unavoidable tariff risks to MMM's robust results from the ongoing, new product launches, with the management guiding 350 new launches in 2026 (+23.2% YoY).

The aggressive portfolio renewal has been successful in driving renewed top-line growth, with "sales from products launched in the last 5 years were up 23% in the full year, exceeding our high-teens target and exited Q4 at 44%, giving us momentum into 2026."

This is especially true since MMM's existing/new offerings for the Safety and Industrial Business Group (SIBG) and Transportation and Electronics Business Group (TEBG) have been instrumental in driving their decent growth at +3.2% YoY and +2% YoY, respectively, against the softer macro environment at stagnant growth.

This is thanks to their ability to tap into the ongoing grid modernization and data center/semiconductor capex boom, with the management already hinting at further growth acceleration in 2026 beyond the +2.7% YoY observed in SIBG's and TEBG's performance in FY2025.

This is on top of MMM's Consumer electrical segment expected to return to growth in FY2026, based on the robust insights observed through the double digits YoY growth observed in December 2025 and "early in January, again, it's early this year, we're looking okay," aided by the easier YoY comparison from -0.3% YoY in FY2025.

Much of the Consumer electrical tailwinds are attributed to the growing launches of premium foldable devices by numerous Chinese and Asian OEMs, as the management also hints at incremental share gains in the mainstream foldable devices market through 2026.

As a result of the promising new launches expected to bring forth "$1B (top-line) growth over macro" and the expanding operating margins, I can understand why the consensus forward estimates remain rather promising, with MMM expected to report a robust top/bottom-line growth at a 3Y CAGR of +3.6%/+7.8% through FY2028, respectively.

This is compared to the 3Y pandemic levels of +0.5%/+1.9% (between FY2020 and FY2023, pre divestiture in April 2024) and the 5Y pre-pandemic levels of +0.2%/+3.5% (between FY2014 and FY2019), respectively, with it underscoring its renewed growth profile aided by the Solventum (SOLV) divestiture of the slower growth, health care business.

As a result of the recent stock price correction and the FY2025 outperformance, MMM now trades relatively compellingly at my estimated FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.15x.

This is down from the recent highs of 21.21x, the sector median of 22.55x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 20.10x, while nearing the 3Y pandemic mean of 16.89x pre-SOLV divestiture.

Based on the estimated FY2026 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.15x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +7.8%, it is apparent that MMM still looks reasonably valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.32x.

This is compared to the sector median of 2.03x, the 3Y pandemic mean of 8.88x pre-SOLV divestiture, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 5.74x, with a similar conclusion also derived after comparing to its industrial peers, including:

  • DuPont de Nemours (DD) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 25.32x/my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.51x and
  • Honeywell International (HON) at 22.37x/4.30x, respectively,

... with it apparent that the overreaction to MMM's mixed FY2026 guidance has been a boon for those looking to add, thanks to the renewed growth opportunities from the SOLV divestiture and the accelerated, strategic product launches in high growth verticals.

MMM Remains A Great Buy Here

For now, MMM has remained relatively well-supported along its 100/200 day moving averages since the Q4'23/Q1'24 bottom, with it signaling a potential trading floor at the intermediate support levels of the $152s.

A similar conclusion may also be observed in MMM's technical indicators across the reasonable RSIs and trading volumes, with it offering interested investors an excellent dip buying opportunity.

This is especially since the stock is now trading below my fair value estimates of $162, based on the FY2026 adj EPS performance of $8.06 (+10.4% YoY) and the 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 20.10x (cheap compared to the sector median of ~22x).

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $9.85, there remains an excellent upside potential of +26.7% to my long-term price target of $197.90 as well.

Readers may also look forward to MMM's potential dividend hike from the current annualized sum of $2.92 per share, based on the last hike by +4.3% in February 2025, with a similar hike by +4.3% likely to bring forth higher payouts of ~$3.04 per share and the expanded forward dividend yield of ~1.9%, up from the current levels of 1.74%.

This is on top of the ongoing share repurchases, with the -1.3% of its float retired over the LTM, the -7.4% since FY2019, and the planned purchases worth ~$2.5B in FY2026 underscoring the management's excellent capital allocation beyond the healthier balance sheet with lower net debts of $7.37B (-26.9% from pre-SOLV divestiture net debt levels of $10.09B in FY2023).

As a result of the improved margin of safety arising from the recent correction/the cheaper valuations/the expanded upside potential, the robust shareholder returns across dividend incomes and share repurchases, along with the healthier balance sheet, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the MMM stock here.

data.title: Tesla: Clear Skies In 2026, But A Storm Could Cut Valuation In 2027
data.description:
It has been a long journey since January 2015, when I first covered Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). With the stock at a split-adjusted ~$12/share, I argued that short-selling the brand was a mistake and that Tesla was instead a contrarian Buy. We all know how that turned out: a precarious startup in electric cars transformed into an S&P 500 (SP500) juggernaut, with a market capitalization of $1.39T. It is easy to overlook that in 2015, similarly to today, Tesla had a valuation that seemed out of touch with reality. At the time, there were many calls to short the company, and my first article was primarily predicated on the argument that shorting a brand that is executing on a dream is potentially a disastrous financial decision.

I didn't cover Tesla again until April 2024, again rating the stock a Buy, although I was perplexed by how far it had fallen and wondered aloud if I should have hit the Sell button. However, I accepted the vicissitudes of the market and found a reason to be bullish again, upgrading to Strong Buy after test driving full self-driving, or FSD, on my Model 3. However, this time I was not lulled to sleep by dreams of unsustainable gains; instead, I rang a bell at the top when I took profits and downgraded Tesla to Hold as the stock rallied to $463/share, arguing in Dec. 2024 that "rapid growth is priced in."

In the year or so since, Tesla stock has fallen by 7% versus a 13% increase in the S&P 500, so what is now priced into the stock? The investment thesis in Tesla is complicated by the company's complexity. To answer this question, we cannot evaluate Tesla as a single entity. Instead, it must be seen as a sum of its parts, including an automotive business, an energy business, and a growth portfolio, consisting of full self-driving, Robotaxi, and Optimus.

Writing this article, I initially expected that I would be bearish on Tesla. With FSD and Robotaxi not contributing much to earnings and Optimus a lofty growth driver in the distant future, I was expecting to find growth targets in 2026 that could not be met without a massive Robotaxi roll-out. Surprisingly, this is not the case: in fact, short- and medium-term targets seem very attainable.

While Tesla's valuation around $435/share appears to leave no margin for error, the analyst community has back-loaded growth, leaving the company with a very strong chance to meet consensus estimates in the coming quarter and year. With respect to the upcoming Q4 2025 earnings (January 28th), it is more likely that the stock will rally than crash, owing to reasonable estimates that will be discussed in the section below. However, investors are paying up-front for a revolution that has not happened yet.

Thus, my cautious stance is to rate Tesla stock a Hold. To put new money to work, lower prices or faster execution on Robotaxi/FSD are required. However, I consider this cautious stance to be short-term bullish as softness in shares and low expectations set the stage wherein the next leg of the stock is more likely up than down.

Tesla: A Sum of the Parts Valuation

To understand Tesla as a business, we must first separate and analyze its segments. Below is a table from Tesla's Q3 2025 10-Q report; it is somewhat counterintuitive that FSD revenue is counted within the automotive sales segment. From the report, the exact monthly revenue of FSD is unclear, although Tesla reports $3.83B in deferred revenue from FSD (among other things), increasing from $3.60B in Q4 2024. This reinforces the conclusion that FSD recurring revenue has not become material enough to warrant its own line item. Recently, Musk disclosed the FSD will only be available as a $99/month subscription as of February, rather than a possible $8,000 standalone purchase.

The "services and other" segment is also slightly counterintuitive, again because it does not include the SaaS contribution of FSD. Instead, it includes non-warranty services, used vehicle sales, supercharging, and insurance.

Therefore, for the purpose of our sum of the parts, Tesla's business is sorted into three segments:

  1. The Legacy Auto Business (Including FSD): Tesla's automotive business is a mature cash cow, facing cyclical headwinds. This includes automotive sales ($20.35B), regulatory credits ($417M), services and other ($3.48B), and automotive leasing ($429M). The total legacy business is therefore $24.7B, or 87.8% of revenues. YoY, this segment grew by 8.3%.
  2. The Energy Business: A fast-growing juggernaut that has quietly become Tesla's growth engine. This includes energy generation and storage sales ($3.28B) and energy generation and storage leasing ($134M). In total, the energy business achieved $3.41B in Q3 sales, or 12.2%, while expanding quickly at 43.5% YoY.
  3. The Future Business: Robotaxi and Optimus. Products that promise billions but remain in the early stages of monetization. They are not yet contributors to Tesla's sales.

With these three segments in mind, let us consider their value. To begin, it is impressive that despite slowing deliveries and declining automotive credits, the legacy business was still able to swing to 8.3% YoY growth in Q3. The legacy auto business run rate is approximately $83.97B (87.8% of TTM revenue). To assign a value, I used the most optimistic possible assumption: imagine that Tesla's automotive business were priced in the most favorable manner in the automotive industry. That would be Ferrari (RACE), which trades for 7.14x 2025 sales. This admittedly optimistic assumption places the legacy auto business at a value of approximately $600B.

Next, consider the energy business. At 12.2% of current revenues, this implies a run rate of $11.66B. A generous 8x sales multiple would imply a valuation of $93B.

Finally, we have Robotaxi and Optimus. These are essentially impossible to value except in terms of total addressable market (TAM). Let us make the very optimistic assumption that Robotaxi is commercialized beginning in 2027 and secures sufficient market share to justify an equivalent market capitalization to Uber (UBER), the largest ride-hailing company in the world. This would value Robotaxi at $175B, with the remaining value of Tesla's shares coming from Optimus. While the TAM for Optimus should be massive, its mass production has been delayed until 2027 but almost certainly will require until 2028 or later.

Optimus, together with Robotaxi, supplies a "growth premium" that represents a substantial part of Tesla's market capitalization at 50.1%. In total, even with highly optimistic assumptions for the legacy business, half of Tesla's valuation is entirely theoretical, based on products that have yet to launch.

Business Segment* ~TTM Revenue Valuation Multiple Implied Value Value per Share Percent Value
Legacy Auto & Services $83.97B 7.14x Sales $599.5B $186 43.2%
Energy $11.66B 8.0x Sales $93B $29 6.7%
Robotaxi -- -- $175B $54 12.5%
Optimus -- -- $522.5B $162 37.6%
Total $1,390B $431 100%

*Tesla: Sum of the Parts Valuation - Author's Estimate.

Now we have sized up Tesla and approximated what investors are paying for each segment. The purpose is to evaluate whether shares are a good purchase at the current market price. Some bulls may argue that Robotaxi will eclipse Uber, or that Optimus will eclipse the legacy auto business. That is possible, and if you think so, the table above provides a good reference for how that could add value to the stock. As I said in January 2015, Tesla's future is very uncertain, and that could be quite bullish.

Tesla: Outlook over the Short, Medium, and Long Term

Nevertheless, these future values will require vast growth. The further this growth lies in the future, the larger it will need to be as an appropriate discount rate is applied.

Here is how the business will be judged in the short, medium, and long term:

Short Term (Q4 2025 Earnings on 1/28/26): The bar for the next earnings report is low. With consensus delivery estimates falling to ~418k vehicles, the market is bracing for a miss. However, analysts have set revenue targets for the quarter of only $24.75B, 3.7% below the $25.71B achieved in Q4 2024. Given growth in the energy segment, these numbers are achievable, perhaps even conservative.

On January 2nd, data was released showing 418,227 vehicles were delivered in Q4 2025, a 15.6% YoY decline versus 495,570 in Q4 2024. This stark number is why estimates have declined and presumably why the stock has moved lower in recent weeks. In Q4 2024, Tesla sold $20.0B of autos, an ASP of $43,200. This would imply $18.06B in sales in Q4 2025.

However, the other item confirmed by the January 2nd release was that energy deployed 14.2 GWh of storage products in Q4. This is a massive surge since, according to the Q3 2025 10-Q, "32.5 GWh of energy storage products [were sold] through the third quarter." Thus, in the first 9 months of 2025, 32.5 GWh corresponded to $8.55B in sales. Correspondingly, we should expect $3.74B in Q4 2024. Let's imagine that the other smaller segments of Tesla's sales were either constant from Q3 or the average of performance for the first 9 months of 2025 (whichever is lower).

This leads to the following estimate for Q4 2025:

Total Revenues $25,827 $28,095
Tesla: Q4 2025 Predictions by Segment* Q4 2025 Q3 2025
Automotive Sales $18,060 $20,359
Automotive Regulatory Credits $417 $417
Energy Generation and Storage Sales $3,740 $3,281
Services and Other $3,053 $3,475
Total Revenue from Sales and Services $25,270 $27,532
Automotive Leasing $429 $429
Energy Generation and Storage Leasing $128 $134

*Tesla's Q4 2025 Performance - Author's Estimate.

In Q4, analysts are expecting $24.75B in revenue, leading to $94.66B for the full year. However, with $69.93B in sales through Q3, our estimate of $25.83B would represent a 4.4% beat on the top line for Q4 and $95.76B for 2025, a slight beat of 1.2%.

My conclusion is that in the short term, Tesla is positioned for a modest beat when it reports earnings on Jan. 28th. However, the legacy auto business is still shrinking versus a year ago. In Q4 2024, Tesla reported $25.71B in sales; thus, if the estimate above proves correct, sales will be flat YoY. The energy business is effectively masking weakness in the auto business. With the stock down 12.2% since all-time highs in Dec., I am moderately bullish, expecting the stock to stabilize in the coming quarter and perhaps trend higher.

Tesla: Analysts Projections for Revenue Growth in the Medium to Long Term

Medium Term (2026): This year, I had assumed that Tesla needed a huge Robotaxi launch to meet growth estimates. However, analysts are anticipating $107.4B in sales, a 13.5% growth rate. This is achievable with energy and FSD sustaining growth, with the automotive business stabilizing. A Robotaxi revolution is not being priced into 2026 estimates by the analyst community.

Based on our estimates for Q4, energy would reach $12.29B in 2025 sales. If its 43.5% YoY growth continues, this segment can reach $17.64B in 2026 sales. This is $5.35B in new revenue. Services & others have grown in a stable fashion, at a 25% growth rate on 2025 revenue of $14B; a contribution of $3.5B in new revenue is achievable.

Where things get somewhat more difficult is that the legacy auto business needs to stabilize in the next 12 months and return to growth in order to meet analyst estimates in 2026. Analysts see $107.39B in revenues, or $11.63B in new revenue. With energy supplying $5.35B and services $3.5B, $2.78B in growth is required for the legacy auto business. This, in a nutshell, is why my outlook for Tesla gets cloudier the further into the future we go. The math says we either need a turnaround in the auto business to justify analyst growth projection or another catalyst, such as accelerated Robotaxi deployment, will be needed to distract the market from the near-term underperformance.

Long Term (2027+): This is where the math becomes very hard, with 2027 and 2028 consensus estimates demanding growth to re-accelerate by 17% and 21%, respectively. This growth cannot mathematically be satisfied by cars, energy, or FSD. It requires a successful launch of a Robotaxi fleet. By 2028, Tesla will need to add $57.7B in revenue, eclipsing Uber's (UBER) 2025 sales. For comparison, Elon Musk's pay package had the goal of 10M FSD subscriptions. This sounds immense; however, $99/month for 10M subscriptions is $11.88B per year. Perhaps that could help plug the hole in 2026 if subscription growth expands, but it is woefully insufficient in 2027 and massively low beyond that. Long-term buyers are betting that execution on Robotaxi will come through next year.

After 2028, Optimus has to kick in to turbocharge growth further. Based on DCF valuation, Tesla is likely worth $276/share, assuming 14.4% revenue growth for 5 years, 10.7% until year 20, and 3% growth in perpetuity. It is impossible to believe the company is not overvalued by 50% at the present time, and sooner or later, Optimus and Robotaxi will need to deliver, or else the stock will decline substantially.

Conclusions

Tesla is a polarizing stock, perhaps more so than almost any stock on the market today. This is because bears see a shrinking automotive business in cyclical decline, propped up (for now) by huge growth in energy that still cannot fully justify the current market price. Bulls, on the other hand, see a huge first-mover advantage in robotics and ride-hailing with Optimus and Robotaxi potentially supplying a huge TAM once their respective commercialization roll-outs gain steam.

I, on the other hand, am caught in between. I have been captivated by Tesla's fantastic progress in FSD; however, I am unsure if this will lead to true unassisted driving. In addition, Tesla has specified that a substantial reduction in the cost of production is required for optimal unit economics for Robotaxi. Clearly, there are many challenges that must be overcome. Optimus remains a "blue-sky" opportunity, and we don't know what the demand will be for a $20-$30k humanoid robot - even if Tesla can make good on its production timeline.

For the time being, my outlook on Tesla is a Hold rating with a bullish short-term bias. In Q4 2025, analyst estimates are likely too low, setting up a potential beat on earnings next week. However, the further into the future we go, the more cloudy the outlook becomes. Investors who want upside in AI, robotics, and robotaxis may be attracted to Tesla at these prices; however, they should remember that further upside is speculative at these levels.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Retracts
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, both neutral sentiment and pessimism increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 6.3 percentage points to 43.2%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the eighth time in 11 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 1.8 percentage points to 24.1%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 79th time in 81 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 4.5 percentage points to 32.7%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 47th time in 52 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 10.8 percentage points to 10.4%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the 12th time in 51 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what their performance forecast for the S&P 500 index is in 2026.

Here is how they responded:

  • Up 10% or more: 25.6%
  • Up between 2% and 9%: 43.5%
  • Flat between +1% and –1%: 12.1%
  • Down between 2% and 9%: 10.3%
  • Down 10% or more: 7.2%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 43.2%, down 6.3 percentage points
  • Neutral: 24.1%, up 1.8 percentage points
  • Bearish: 32.7%, up 4.5 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.
